{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27473","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27473/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27473/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27473/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27473","id":2555161765,"node_id":"I_kwDOAA3dP86YTKyl","number":27473,"title":"BUG: Incorrect Data Type Promotion When Using Multiple Threads","user":{"login":"markgras","id":17365607,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3MzY1NjA3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/17365607?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/markgras","html_url":"https://github.com/markgras","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/markgras/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/markgras/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/markgras/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/markgras/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/markgras/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/markgras/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/markgras/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/markgras/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/markgras/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":6359214,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw2MzU5MjE0","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/00%20-%20Bug","name":"00 - Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-09-29T21:22:38Z","updated_at":"2024-09-29T21:22:38Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

Thanks for the great project! NumPy 2.0 introduced changes to NumPy data type promotion per [NEP 50](https://numpy.org/neps/nep-0050-scalar-promotion.html). To opt out of these changes, a developer can set promotion state to `legacy`. When working with threads, it seems as though promotion state is not consistent. In the following example, the main thread uses the new promotion rules, but the second thread uses legacy promotion rules. This appears to be the behavior of both NumPy 2.1.0 and 2.1.1. In 2.0.0 thru 2.0.2, the second thread uses the new promotion rules. I can recreate this behavior with multiple CPython versions on multiple Linux distros.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
from threading import Thread
import numpy as np

def func(name):
    x = np.uint8(100) + 200
    print(name, x, type(x))

func("Main thread")
t = Thread(target=func, args=("Second thread",))
t.start()
t.join()
```


### Error message:

```shell
When I run the above code example, here is the output:

/home/mark/issue.py:5: RuntimeWarning: overflow encountered in scalar add
  x = np.uint8(100) + 200
Main thread 44 <class 'numpy.uint8'>
Second thread 300 <class 'numpy.int64'>
```
```


### Python and NumPy Versions:

2.1.1
3.12.5 (main, Aug  9 2024, 08:20:41) [GCC 14.2.1 20240805]

### Runtime Environment:

[{'numpy_version': '2.1.1',
  'python': '3.12.5 (main, Aug  9 2024, 08:20:41) [GCC 14.2.1 20240805]',
  'uname': uname_result(system='Linux', node='device', release='6.10.6-10-MANJARO', version='#1 SMP PREEMPT_DYNAMIC Sat Aug 24 16:17:40 UTC 2024', machine='x86_64')},
 {'simd_extensions': {'baseline': ['SSE', 'SSE2', 'SSE3'],
                      'found': ['SSSE3',
                                'SSE41',
                                'POPCNT',
                                'SSE42',
                                'AVX',
                                'F16C',
                                'FMA3',
                                'AVX2'],
                      'not_found': ['AVX512F',
                                    'AVX512CD',
                                    'AVX512_KNL',
                                    'AVX512_KNM',
                                    'AVX512_SKX',
                                    'AVX512_CLX',
                                    'AVX512_CNL',
                                    'AVX512_ICL']}},
 {'architecture': 'Haswell',
  'filepath': '/home/mark/venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/numpy.libs/libscipy_openblas64_-ff651d7f.so',
  'internal_api': 'openblas',
  'num_threads': 16,
  'prefix': 'libscipy_openblas',
  'threading_layer': 'pthreads',
  'user_api': 'blas',
  'version': '0.3.27'}]

### Context for the issue:

This was a rather hard issue to track down. The legacy promotion state is silently enabled in the second thread, the legacy rules are to silently allow changes to data types, and this behavior is inconsistent across NumPy versions. In 1.26.x, both threads use legacy rules. In 2.0.x, both threads use the new rules. In 2.1.x, each thread uses different rules. I discovered this issue only after migrating from 1.26 to 2.1, and then downgrading to 2.0 later on to appease a new dependency. Code that behaves variably is hard to debug! ### Expected behavior

I expect [Figure.image](https://docs.bokeh.org/en/latest/docs/reference/plotting/figure.html#bokeh.plotting.figure.image) to accept "vanilla" Python data structures (e.g. tuples and lists) as arguments, as was the case before bokeh v3.0.

### Observed behavior

`Figure.image` renders as expected if passed a list containing a single 2D numpy array as its first argument. However, it does not render anything if said numpy array is replaced with a list of lists (or tuple of tuples, etc.). In this situation, errors are logged to the browser's console. In the example code below, uncommenting the `data = np.array(data)` line will cause the figure to render properly.

### Example code

```Python
import numpy as np
from bokeh.plotting import figure, show

data = [
    [0, 0],
    [1, 1],
]
# data = np.array(data)
p = figure()
p.image(image=[data], x=0, y=0, dw=10, dh=10, palette="Spectral11", level="image")
show(p)
```


### Stack traceback or browser console output

There are no Python errors. Here is the browser's console output:
```
11:49:03.008 [bokeh] setting log level to: 'info' bokeh-3.0.2.min.js:183:1311
11:49:03.054 Uncaught TypeError: can't access property "top_panel", n is undefined
    _measure_layout https://cdn.bokeh.org/bokeh/release/bokeh-3.0.2.min.js:574
    measure_layout https://cdn.bokeh.org/bokeh/release/bokeh-3.0.2.min.js:539

    _resize_observer https://cdn.bokeh.org/bokeh/release/bokeh-3.0.2.min.js:425
bokeh-3.0.2.min.js:574:8245
11:49:03.055 Uncaught (in promise) Error: expected a 2D array
    t https://cdn.bokeh.org/bokeh/release/bokeh-3.0.2.min.js:177
    assert https://cdn.bokeh.org/bokeh/release/bokeh-3.0.2.min.js:177

I was unaware of this change. I do not see a mention of it in the v3.0.0 [migration guide](https://github.com/bokeh/bokeh/wiki/Migration-Guides#300), nor in the current `Figure.image` [docs](https://docs.bokeh.org/en/latest/docs/reference/plotting/figure.html#bokeh.plotting.figure.image).

Here's my two cents, for whatever it's worth: I believe that the best way to handle this is to validate arguments and raise a `TypeError` in such a case. I was stumped by this issue for quite a while, and the presence of only browser error logs made me think that this was an internal Bokeh issue. Although I agree that input validation slows things down, at the end of the day developers are using Python for its ease of use and not for its performance.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/bokeh/bokeh/issues/comments/1315713987/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}
